fields.bio: Backend developer from Tashkent. Django, Python, Linux. CTF enthusiast.
UI/UX designer. I write about design systems, accessibility, and creativity.
DevOps engineer. Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines.
Frontend developer. Vue, React, and everything CSS.
Full-stack developer. I build things and break them to learn.

fields.title: Getting Started with Django
fields.body:
Django is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design.

Install Django:

    pip install django
    django-admin startproject mysite
    cd mysite
    python manage.py runserver

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 and you'll see the welcome page. From here you create apps, define models, wire URLs, and build templates. That's the Django way.

fields.title: Why I Use Linux as My Daily Driver
fields.body:
Three years ago I switched from Windows to Linux. I haven't looked back.

Full control over your system, native terminal, package managers, no bloatware. As a developer everything just works — Docker, Python, SSH, Nginx — without workarounds.

For security research it's even better. Tools like nmap, burpsuite, metasploit — all native.

fields.title: Python Tips I Wish I Knew Earlier
fields.body:
After writing Python daily, these small tricks made the biggest difference.

Use enumerate instead of range(len()), f-strings over .format(), list comprehensions, and context managers. They make code more readable and less error-prone.

fields.title: Minimalism in Web Design
fields.body:
Minimalism isn't just aesthetic — it's a philosophy. Remove everything that doesn't serve the user.

A good font, comfortable line height, and proper spacing carries a design further than any fancy UI element. Minimalist sites also load fast. Fast sites keep users.

fields.title: Design Systems: Why Every Team Needs One
fields.body:
A design system is a single source of truth for your product's UI. It includes components, tokens, patterns, and documentation.

Without a design system, every developer makes slightly different buttons. With one, consistency is automatic.

Start small: define your color tokens, typography scale, and spacing. Build components on top of those. Document everything.

fields.title: Accessibility Is Not Optional
fields.body:
About 15% of the world has some form of disability. If your site doesn't support keyboard navigation, screen readers, or proper color contrast — you're excluding millions.

Accessibility best practices: use semantic HTML, provide alt text for images, ensure 4.5:1 contrast ratio, make everything keyboard-navigable.

It's not extra work — it's good engineering.

fields.title: Docker in 10 Minutes
fields.body:
Docker lets you package your application with all its dependencies into a container that runs the same everywhere.

    # Build
    docker build -t myapp .

    # Run
    docker run -p 8000:8000 myapp

Key concepts: images, containers, volumes, networks. Learn these and you'll never have 'works on my machine' problems again.

fields.title: CI/CD Pipelines with GitHub Actions
fields.body:
Continuous Integration and Deployment automates your software delivery. GitHub Actions makes it easy.

Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml and define triggers, jobs, and steps. On every push to main: run tests, build Docker image, deploy to server.

Free for public repos, affordable for private. No reason not to automate.

fields.title: CSS Grid vs Flexbox: When to Use Which
fields.body:
Both are layout tools, but they solve different problems.

Flexbox is one-dimensional — it arranges items in a row or column. Use it for nav bars, button groups, card rows.

Grid is two-dimensional — it manages rows AND columns simultaneously. Use it for page layouts, image galleries, dashboards.

Rule of thumb: component layout → Flexbox. Page layout → Grid.

fields.title: Vue 3 Composition API: A Practical Guide
Building REST APIs with Django REST Framework
PostgreSQL vs SQLite: Choosing the Right Database
fields.body:
SQLite is perfect for development and small apps. Zero config, file-based, fast for reads.

PostgreSQL shines in production: concurrent writes, full-text search, JSON support, robust ACID compliance.

My rule: SQLite for prototypes and personal projects, PostgreSQL for anything you'd put on a server.
